title: Chicklet

jokes.text: American University sounds like a made-up college in Egypt.
jokes.setup: Amy suggests American University as a venue for Selina's presidential library.
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses it as sounding like a fictitious institution in Egypt, with the specificity of 'Egypt' landing as the absurd flourish.
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina snobbery delivered with perfect economy. The specificity of 'Egypt' is what elevates it from a generic put-down to a sharp character moment — it's exactly how Selina's dismissive, geographically snobbish mind works. Strong opener.

jokes.text: No, I heard her. You understand that, Amy, that even Richard has trouble understanding you?
jokes.setup: Amy repeats what she said because Selina seemed not to hear. Selina clarifies she heard fine.
jokes.punchline: Selina uses the moment to insult Amy's communication by citing Richard — the show's lovable dimwit — as a baseline for comprehension.
jokes.explanation: Works because it inverts the expectation (Selina heard fine) and lands an insult using Richard as a comic measuring stick. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: Even that sanctimonious fraud Carter had a library.
jokes.setup: Selina is lamenting that every president gets a library.
jokes.punchline: Carter is singled out as a 'sanctimonious fraud' — a characterization of a widely-respected ex-president that reveals Selina's bitterness and pettiness.
jokes.explanation: The specific venom aimed at Carter — a man famous for his moral rectitude — perfectly illuminates Selina's jealous, status-obsessed worldview. 'Sanctimonious fraud' is a strong two-word character assassination.

jokes.text: American University, it's like American Univer-shit.
jokes.setup: Continuing her disdain for American University as a library site.
jokes.punchline: The childish portmanteau 'Univer-shit' — funny precisely because it's so juvenilely crass coming from a former president.
jokes.explanation: A slightly cheap wordplay gag, but the character context saves it. A former POTUS doing playground-level name substitution is its own joke. Functional but not sophisticated.

jokes.text: Selina fumbling the name of National Volunteer Week mid-speech, needing to be prompted, then continuing with hollow enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving what should be a gracious public speech.
jokes.punchline: She can't remember the name of the event she's speaking at and needs a staffer to whisper it — then continues as if nothing happened with 'Yeah, really important.'
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep cringe — Selina is performing sincerity she doesn't feel for a cause she doesn't remember. The hollow 'Yeah, really important' after being fed the name is a perfect character beat.

jokes.text: Get this T-shirt off of me. I look like I'm trying out for 'The Price is Right.'
jokes.setup: Selina was just warmly thanking volunteers while wearing an event T-shirt.
jokes.punchline: The instant mask-off — the enthusiastic public persona drops, replaced by Selina comparing herself to a game show contestant.
jokes.explanation: The whiplash from public warmth to private contempt is a Veep signature move and this executes it perfectly. 'The Price is Right' is specific, visual, and degrading in exactly the right register for Selina's status anxiety.

jokes.text: God Almighty, I wish I'd been assassinated in office.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting about her post-presidential indignities.
jokes.punchline: She wishes for her own assassination as preferable to the current situation — immediate dark escalation.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely dark and specific to Selina's character — she'd rather be dead and famous than alive and diminished. The casual delivery makes it land harder. High quotability.

jokes.text: Although, with my luck, I'd be then crippled for life, sitting in a wheelchair in my reading room with my name on the thing.
jokes.setup: Gary reacts with horror to Selina wishing she'd been assassinated.
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately undercuts her own dark wish by imagining the worst-case scenario — surviving but disabled, while still noting she doesn't even have a library with her name on it.
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates by having Selina find the dark side of her own dark wish, and then sneaks in the callback to the library naming dispute. Multi-layered and very much in Selina's voice — she can't even fantasize without finding the downside.

jokes.text: They haven't agreed to putting your name on anything, ma'am. [reaction beat — Selina's stunned silence/outrage]
jokes.setup: Selina's dark joke about being in a wheelchair 'with my name on the thing' sets up an expectation that the library naming is settled.
jokes.punchline: Amy flatly reveals they haven't agreed to put her name on anything — puncturing even her assassination fantasy.
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan undercut. The setup was Selina imagining the worst-case scenario of her assassination fantasy, and Amy calmly makes it worse. The comic structure is airtight — the punchline arrives exactly when the audience has just processed the previous joke.

jokes.text: Oh, God, I hate every food ever, from everywhere.
jokes.setup: Gary asks if Selina wants anything after she didn't eat.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response expands a simple 'no thank you' into a sweeping declaration of hatred for all food from all places and all times.
jokes.explanation: The existential totality of 'every food ever, from everywhere' is a small gem. Establishes her post-heart-attack depression spiral through comic hyperbole.

jokes.text: What?! They have me next to Gerald Ford? Is this the Hall of Half-Term Wonders?
jokes.setup: Gary reveals that Selina's wax figure at Madame Tussauds has been unveiled.
jokes.punchline: She's placed next to Gerald Ford and coins the phrase 'Hall of Half-Term Wonders' — calling out both Ford (never elected president) and herself (didn't complete her term).
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the cold open stretch and one of the episode's finest. 'Hall of Half-Term Wonders' is an instantly iconic coinage — it's funny as a political insult to Ford, devastating as a self-own (Selina also didn't complete her term), and perfectly encapsulates Veep's ability to have Selina be simultaneously the victim and the perpetrator of status anxiety. Extremely quotable, character-defining, and politically sharp.

jokes.text: I thought that was Craig T. Nelson.
jokes.setup: Selina is appalled by being placed next to Gerald Ford's wax figure.
jokes.punchline: Gary's solution to the insult is to confess he thought the Ford wax figure was Craig T. Nelson — a C-list celebrity comparison for a US President.
jokes.explanation: Gary's oblivious, slightly dim take on the Ford figure is perfect in its specificity — Craig T. Nelson is exactly the right level of forgettable semi-celebrity. The joke works because it doubles as another insult to Ford.

jokes.text: Why do they even have a wax figure of Ford? The real one was made of wood.
jokes.setup: Selina asks why Madame Tussauds even has a Ford wax figure.
jokes.punchline: The real Ford was 'made of wood' — a double-meaning insult (wooden personality) that also lands as a physical absurdist joke about wax vs. wood.
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical — five words deliver both a political insult and a meta-joke about the absurdity of a wax figure of someone who was already perceived as stiff and robotic. High quotability.

jokes.text: ...melt down that whole collection and use it to wax Madame Tussauds' big, fat, nasty snatch!
jokes.setup: Selina wants to be moved next to Lincoln or Mao — 'game-changing icons.'
jokes.punchline: Her threat escalates from merely moving the figures to melting the entire collection and using the wax for a grotesque purpose.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from reasonable demand to nuclear obscenity is perfectly Selina. The wordplay on 'wax' (wax figure/waxing) is the structural spine, and the delivery of 'big, fat, nasty snatch' lands as an absurd peak. Very quotable.

jokes.text: My pantyhose go up so high. [non-sequitur physical complaint immediately after Selina's tirade]
jokes.setup: Selina just delivered a threatening, profane tirade about Madame Tussauds.
jokes.punchline: She immediately deflates into a mundane complaint about her pantyhose — the bathos of following a tirade with hosiery discomfort.
jokes.explanation: A textbook Veep bathos beat — the tirade punctured by physical indignity. Works as a character comedy grace note showing that even mid-rage, Selina is uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Trust me, Amy, it was not the heart attack that depressed your dad.
jokes.setup: Amy shares that her father went through a depression after a heart attack and was 'unbearable.'
jokes.punchline: Selina implies Amy herself was the reason for her father's depression — a cutting, character-specific insult.
jokes.explanation: This is a precision strike disguised as a throwaway. Selina pivots Amy's empathetic anecdote into a devastating put-down in one sentence. The structure is elegant — Amy sets up the depression anecdote, Selina recontextualizes the cause. Very Veep: cruelty delivered with surgical economy.

jokes.text: I just feel like I'm a thick, dark fog and everyone disappoints me and nothing works out, and what's the point of anything anyway? And before you ask, it's not because I'm not sleeping, okay? 'Cause I'm getting 14 hours of sleep a night.
jokes.setup: Selina insists she's not depressed.
jokes.punchline: She then delivers a textbook description of depression, followed by defensively volunteering that she's sleeping 14 hours a night — which also indicates depression.
jokes.explanation: The joke is structurally perfect: Selina insists she's not depressed, describes depression, then offers evidence of depression as a counter-argument. The 14 hours of sleep detail is the killer specific. Strong irony.

jokes.text: Wendy and I went to see her when things were rocky. Both of them went to town on me. It was brutal. I cried so hard, I threw up, but it was the best thing I've ever done.
jokes.setup: Mike recommends his therapist and explains he went with his wife Wendy.
jokes.punchline: The escalating description of the therapy session — 'went to town on me,' throwing up from crying — delivered with Mike's characteristic oblivious sincerity, treating this as a ringing endorsement.
jokes.explanation: Mike's hapless earnestness is well-deployed here. The joke is that he's describing a horror as a glowing recommendation, with each detail making it sound worse. 'I cried so hard, I threw up' as a selling point is a strong Mike beat.

jokes.text: You're in therapy? Since when? / Since I was 13. / Well, what you really needed was a dermatologist.
jokes.setup: Selina is surprised to learn Catherine has been in therapy.
jokes.punchline: Instead of engaging with the emotional weight of her daughter's long-term mental health treatment, Selina pivots to a dig about Catherine's teenage skin.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest lines. It perfectly encapsulates the Selina-Catherine dynamic in one sentence: Selina can't engage with her daughter's emotional needs, redirecting to a superficial appearance criticism. 'Dermatologist' is the perfectly chosen word — specific, cutting, and revealing. Strong contender for episode highlight.

jokes.text: Well, my father and I both hate eating butterscotch, but love to say it. Butterscotch.
jokes.setup: Catherine asks Richard about hereditary issues in his family, as he's the sperm donor for their baby.
jokes.punchline: Richard's answer about 'hereditary issues' is a love of saying the word 'butterscotch' — delivered with complete sincerity and a demonstration.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's purest absurdist joke and one of the best Richard moments. The question is medical/serious (hereditary conditions for a donor baby), the answer is a whimsical phonetic preference. The word 'butterscotch' is itself funny to say, so the demonstration lands as its own micro-punchline. Richard's earnestness makes it land twice as hard. Highly quotable, utterly original.

jokes.text: Hey, this isn't a library! / Oh, no, it's a graveyard that sells gum. / Print is dead.
jokes.setup: They're at a newsstand/pharmacy and Amy notes it's not a library.
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'graveyard that sells gum' is a sharp description of a dying newspaper outlet, immediately followed by the meta 'print is dead.'
jokes.explanation: 'A graveyard that sells gum' is a beautiful, precise description of a struggling newsstand. Punchy and quotable. The 'print is dead' follow-up is less original but serves as a second beat.

jokes.text: No, I get all my gum on Amazon.
jokes.setup: Someone at the newsstand offers gum for sale.
jokes.punchline: Dan casually reveals he buys gum on Amazon — a single throwaway line that captures modern consumer absurdity and Dan's insufferable tech-forward pretension.
jokes.explanation: A crisp observational gag. The specificity of 'gum on Amazon' (not books or something sensible) is the joke — and it fits Dan's character as someone who performs sophistication through convenience tech.

jokes.text: Just for tax purposes, why don't you make it out to cash? That's easy, hmm? / I'll just leave it blank.
jokes.setup: Andrew is being questioned about why a charity check for the Rare Bird Conservancy needs to go through the Andrew H. Meyer Trust.
jokes.punchline: Andrew's 'just make it out to cash' is a transparently fraudulent instruction, and Catherine's 'I'll just leave it blank' is an innocent misunderstanding that inadvertently makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Andrew's brazen fraudulence played as chipper helpfulness is great character comedy. Catherine's literal response is a nice comic aside. The scene works as gentle corruption comedy.

jokes.text: You asked her to wait by your street meter in case it ran out.
jokes.setup: Andrew drops in unannounced to see his daughters, wondering where 'little Madge' is.
jokes.punchline: Andrew sent his other daughter to watch his parking meter — treating her like an errand-runner rather than a daughter.
jokes.explanation: A clean joke that characterizes Andrew as a man who uses his daughter as a parking attendant. Economy is strong and the specificity of 'street meter' makes it land.

jokes.text: You're not giving any money to the Music Man here, are you, sweetie?
jokes.setup: Selina arrives to find Andrew with their daughters.
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual dismissal of Andrew as 'the Music Man' — the iconic con-man musical reference — perfectly encapsulates her contempt in a single culturally loaded nickname.
jokes.explanation: The Music Man reference is doing a lot of work efficiently: it's a con man musical, it implies Andrew is a charming fraud, and it's delivered with the cheerful viciousness typical of Selina addressing people she despises.

jokes.text: And what chores do you do around the house to deserve that? / I show assholes the door.
jokes.setup: Andrew condescendingly asks what Selina does to deserve her monthly allowance (which she gives Catherine).
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I show assholes the door' is both literally about removing Andrew and a general job description — and she then literally shows him out.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels — as a comeback to Andrew's patronizing question, as Selina's self-description, and as a setup for literally dismissing him. The double meaning is efficient and the delivery must be devastating. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Here's a statement... your investment is doing quite well.
jokes.setup: Mike notes he's never gotten a financial statement from Andrew about his investment.
jokes.punchline: Andrew's response is to verbally tell him 'your investment is doing quite well' — the statement IS the statement, with no actual documentation.
jokes.explanation: A neat, economical joke about financial fraud — Andrew reframes a verbal assurance as the 'statement' Mike requested. The wordplay on 'statement' is clean.

jokes.text: He was fucking Louise Kellog for me, my goddamn press secretary. 'Cause that's what he does, see? He makes you...
jokes.setup: Selina is recounting Andrew's cheating during her first congressional campaign — he claimed to be 'campaigning long hours at the office for her.'
jokes.punchline: 'He was fucking Louise Kellog for me' — the grammatical construction of his affair as something done on her behalf, as if it were campaign service.
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'for me' is the entire joke — Selina has absorbed Andrew's self-serving framing so deeply she retells his betrayal in his language. Sharp character writing with a grammatical twist.

jokes.text: And then we just hired the least fuckable press secretary we could find.
jokes.setup: Selina fired her press secretary after discovering Andrew was sleeping with her.
jokes.punchline: The solution was to hire based on unfuckability — with the implication that Mike is that press secretary.
jokes.explanation: The joke lands in two waves: first the absurdly clinical 'least fuckable' hiring criterion, then the realization that Mike is the result. Mike's reaction beat sells it. One of the stronger jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Yeah, if you sit, it's a meal, which is a gift, which is a bribe, which is a line of prison inmates standing on each other's shoulders trying to sodomize you.
jokes.setup: Jonah wants to sit down at a fundraiser because his feet hurt.
jokes.punchline: The FEC fundraising rules are explained via an escalating chain of logic that ends with a grotesque prison sodomy image — a reductio ad absurdum of campaign finance law.
jokes.explanation: The escalation is perfectly structured: each step follows logically from the last until it careens into complete absurdity. 'A line of prison inmates standing on each other's shoulders trying to sodomize you' is a truly original image for legal jeopardy. Very Veep — turning bureaucratic rules into grotesque comedy.

jokes.text: Hi, Mrs. Tanz. Ms. Tanz, you are smoking. / I'm the daughter. Gross.
jokes.setup: Jonah meets Tanz's wife and daughter simultaneously.
jokes.punchline: He hits on who he thinks is the wife, who is actually the daughter — instant cringe.
jokes.explanation: A reliable cringe beat. Not particularly original (hitting on daughter instead of wife is a well-worn setup), but 'Gross' as the daughter's single-word response is perfectly economical. Jonah's character integrates well with the obliviousness.

jokes.text: More crime means more for-profit prisons. We're gonna lock 'em up and we're gonna fry 'em. / What are you, a monster? / If you kill them, they can't get arrested again. That's how we make our money. / Processing fees.
jokes.setup: Tanz wants Jonah to link permanent Daylight Savings to crime — more darkness = more crime.
jokes.punchline: The 'solution' of executing prisoners is immediately undermined by the realization that dead prisoners generate no processing fees — so the scheme collapses under its own capitalist logic.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark political satire about the prison-industrial complex. The joke neatly exposes the contradiction: for-profit prisons need living prisoners, so killing them is bad for business. 'Processing fees' is a killer button word.

jokes.text: Right, which you would know if you hadn't been losing a battle of wits with a meatball.
jokes.setup: Jonah doesn't understand the basic economics of for-profit prisons despite being at a fundraiser with the prison magnate.
jokes.punchline: Shawnee's 'losing a battle of wits with a meatball' is a precise image for Jonah's intellect.
jokes.explanation: 'Losing a battle of wits with a meatball' is a beautifully original insult — the specificity of 'meatball' (round, soft, brainless, Italian party food) as an intellectual adversary is genuinely creative. Highly quotable and perfectly delivered as Shawnee's introduction as a sharp character foil to Jonah.

jokes.text: Failure to thrive at birth... fuck that. Puberty at 19. When it hit, it hit like a fucking thunderbolt. Third grade reading level in the tenth grade. Everything that I have been through in my entire life has only made me stronger and taller.
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to impress Shawnee by listing everything he's overcome.
jokes.punchline: 'Has only made me stronger and taller' — the list of developmental failures delivered as inspirational triumph, culminating in 'taller' as a non-sequitur achievement.
jokes.explanation: This is one of Jonah's finest character speeches. Each item on the list is funnier than the last, and 'stronger and taller' as the triumphant conclusion is perfect — 'taller' being his actual distinguishing trait, treated as a hard-won achievement alongside overcoming literal childhood development failures. Deep character integration and genuinely original.

jokes.text: I begged Daddy to get her for me, and he did. I remember he said, 'Squirrel, you're an intense little girl, and now you finally have a friend.'
jokes.setup: Selina fondly describes her childhood horse, Chicklet.
jokes.punchline: Her father's gift of a horse is framed as giving his daughter her 'first friend' — a devastating parenthetical character reveal.
jokes.explanation: The nickname 'Squirrel' and the implication that Selina was so socially difficult her father bought her a horse as a substitute friend is both funny and quietly devastating. Strong character-building joke that also doubles as backstory. The warmth in Selina's recounting it makes it more poignant and funnier.

jokes.text: Mother up and sold her. She hated all animals that she couldn't eat or wear.
jokes.setup: Selina is fondly talking about her horse Chicklet.
jokes.punchline: Her mother sold the horse — described with the taxonomy of a woman who only values animals instrumentally.
jokes.explanation: 'She hated all animals that she couldn't eat or wear' is a perfectly economical character sketch of Selina's mother — ruthlessly practical, status-obsessed, and cold. Also previews the later reveal that the horse was actually sold by the father, making this a callback. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Boy, I'll tell you, I remember that night he died 'cause his secretary came banging on the door at, like, 2:00 AM.
jokes.setup: Selina is movingly describing the night her father died.
jokes.punchline: The secretary 'came banging on the door' — initially presented as the professional delivering bad news, but the 2AM timing and nature of the visit plant seeds of a darker revelation.
jokes.explanation: This is a slow-burn misdirection setup — the comedy isn't fully apparent here but on rewatch the audience recognizes this as the seed of the entire Daddy-was-an-adulterer reveal. Functions better structurally than as a standalone joke, but deserves recognition.

jokes.text: Hmm, see? I'm not so bad once you get to know me after 20 years.
jokes.setup: Selina and Mike are having a rare warm moment by the fire.
jokes.punchline: Selina self-congratulates on being 'not so bad' — while acknowledging it took 20 years of working together for this to become true.
jokes.explanation: The joke rests entirely on 'after 20 years' — the admission that it takes two decades before Selina becomes tolerable. Delivers both warmth and self-awareness in her characteristic backhanded way.

jokes.text: What is this, Yonkers? / Just go and get sausage patties.
jokes.setup: Mike asks for sausage patties for his eight-egg-white omelet breakfast.
jokes.punchline: Selina's contemptuous 'What is this, Yonkers?' — implying sausage patties are beneath the sophistication expected at Mee-Maw's estate — then immediately ordering Gary to get them anyway.
jokes.explanation: 'What is this, Yonkers?' is a good geographic snobbery joke — Yonkers as the nadir of suburban uncouthness. The irony that she immediately orders them anyway is a classic Selina contradiction beat.

jokes.text: She hasn't yelled at me in, like, 24 hours. / It's weird.
jokes.setup: Mike and Amy notice Selina has been kinder recently.
jokes.punchline: The baseline for 'good behavior' being 24 hours without yelling — treated as an unusual and unsettling development.
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat that works as a quick laugh. The 24-hour no-yelling window as a measure of kindness says everything about their working relationship.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'Oh, my God. / [to Richard] Oh, you mean masturbate?'
jokes.setup: Richard has never donated sperm before and has never 'shook the devil's hand.'
jokes.punchline: Catherine's blunt translation of Richard's euphemism lands as comic punctuation to his innocence.
jokes.explanation: Catherine's matter-of-fact translation works well as a reaction beat. The comedy is in the clinical frankness versus Richard's quaint religiosity.

jokes.text: Oh, self-husband.
jokes.setup: Catherine translates 'shook the devil's hand' as masturbation.
jokes.punchline: Richard's counter-euphemism 'self-husband' — a gentle, innocently wholesome reframing.
jokes.explanation: 'Self-husband' is a genuine comedy gem. It's a completely original euphemism that could only come from Richard's combination of rural religiosity, innocence, and syntactic creativity. It's instantly iconic, deeply character-specific, and delivered with complete sincerity. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Well, my family in Iowa was pretty religious. Grandma Splett always said that self-pleasure was a sin like microwaves or laughter.
jokes.setup: Richard is explaining his inexperience with masturbation.
jokes.punchline: Grandma Splett's taxonomy of sins includes microwaves and laughter — absurdist escalation that reveals a fundamentalist household through two perfectly chosen, ridiculous examples.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's peak joke. 'Sin like microwaves or laughter' is a piece of absurdist comedy writing at its finest — the two additional sins are specific, escalating, and perfect. 'Microwaves' establishes technophobic fundamentalism; 'laughter' is the devastating escalation that implies a joyless existence. The construction is airtight, character-specific (only Richard could deliver this with sincerity), and endlessly quotable. Rivals the anchor examples for craft.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, I'm still understanding how the cup comes into play.
jokes.setup: Richard has just agreed to provide the sperm donation.
jokes.punchline: He still doesn't understand the basic mechanics — specifically 'how the cup comes into play' — demonstrating an innocence so complete it borders on the miraculous.
jokes.explanation: Richard's procedural confusion about sperm donation — specifically the cup — is a great comedy of innocence beat. 'How the cup comes into play' is the precise, polite way only Richard would phrase this.

jokes.text: No, worse comes to worst, I'll burn in hell like Grandma Splett.
jokes.setup: Someone asks if Richard needs a moment to prepare for the sperm donation.
jokes.punchline: Richard cheerfully accepts the possibility of damnation — while also retroactively applying it to his grandma, suggesting she went to hell.
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback to the Grandma Splett joke — Richard decides to take his chances with eternal damnation, and in doing so reveals that Grandma Splett herself went to hell (for laughter? microwaves?). The cheerful resignation is quintessentially Richard.

jokes.text: How many of these do I need to fill up? / Yeah, no, you just have to do one. / Just one? Okay. I think that'll be easier. / How will I know when I'm done? / You know, I'll figure it out. I'll probably figure it out.
jokes.setup: Richard is being briefed on the sperm donation process.
jokes.punchline: Each question Richard asks reveals more profound inexperience, culminating in 'How will I know when I'm done?' — then his cheerful self-reliance: 'I'll probably figure it out.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating questions build beautifully to 'How will I know when I'm done?' — perhaps the most innocent question imaginable about male physiology. 'I'll probably figure it out' as his solution is perfectly Richard: optimistic, self-sufficient, and blissfully unaware.

jokes.text: Why didn't you take me back to your place? / Do you live with your mom? / No. Pfft, no. She wishes. / I live in my office. DC rents are insane.
jokes.setup: Shawnee asks why Jonah didn't take her to his place after sex.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defensive 'She wishes' before admitting he lives in his congressional office because DC rents are insane.
jokes.explanation: 'She wishes' is a great Jonah beat — his pride at being free of mom before immediately revealing the pathetic truth. The DC rent line makes it feel contemporary and grounded.

jokes.text: We'll fuck again tonight at 7:30 with less kissing.
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to extend the sexual encounter.
jokes.punchline: Shawnee schedules the next encounter with clinical efficiency — 7:30, with a note to reduce kissing — treating it as a business appointment.
jokes.explanation: The scheduling of sex with a specific time and a quality-improvement note ('less kissing') is a perfectly calibrated joke about Shawnee's transactional personality. Highly quotable and great character establishment.

jokes.text: Did my missing ball freak you out? / Honestly, it was one less thing to worry about.
jokes.setup: Jonah nervously asks if his cancer-related missing testicle was an issue.
jokes.punchline: Shawnee's brutally pragmatic 'one less thing to worry about' — treating his medical loss as a sexual convenience.
jokes.explanation: A masterfully dark and perfectly economical joke. The setup earns sympathy for Jonah, then Shawnee obliterates it with a clinical positive spin. 'One less thing to worry about' applied to a missing testicle is a superb deployment of understatement. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Okay. Thanks, Mrs. Tanz. / What? / Ms. Tanz.
jokes.setup: Shawnee reassures Jonah about his missing testicle.
jokes.punchline: Jonah calls her 'Mrs. Tanz' — the married name of her father's wife — immediately after a sexual encounter, implying he can't keep the family members straight.
jokes.explanation: A quick cringe button — Jonah can't remember the basic family relationships of the woman he just slept with. Efficient and characterizes his obliviousness.

jokes.text: Five-minute feud truce. The most amazing thing just happened to me twice!
jokes.setup: Jonah interrupts Richard to share news.
jokes.punchline: 'The most amazing thing just happened to me twice' — the 'twice' specifying that he had sex two times, delivered with the excitement of someone sharing a breakthrough discovery.
jokes.explanation: The 'twice' is doing all the work — it reveals the nature of the 'amazing thing' while characterizing Jonah as someone for whom having sex twice is genuinely remarkable. Crisp and economical.

jokes.text: They're fucking her in the ass? / I have to see this.
jokes.setup: Amy is describing how the Selina wax figure has been repositioned next to FDR, and people are 'taking advantage' of her.
jokes.punchline: Amy's euphemistic concern is immediately translated by Dan into graphic terms, then his 'I have to see this' reveals his enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: Dan's crass translation and enthusiasm is a strong character beat. 'I have to see this' is perfectly Dan — amoral and gleeful at Selina's humiliation.

jokes.text: This girl is, like, 11 years old. Where are her parents? [visual gag — reaction to seeing child posing with the wax figure inappropriately]
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan have gone to see the sexually-posed Selina wax figure.
jokes.punchline: The scene has an 11-year-old child involved in whatever is happening with the statue — escalating the public humiliation to a genuinely disturbing level.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the wax figure degradation to include a child observer adds a disturbing layer. Amy's parental concern in the middle of this outrage is a great reaction beat.

jokes.text: You're as useless as a dick at a roller derby.
jokes.setup: Gary failed to fix the wax figure situation.
jokes.punchline: Amy's insult uses a vivid, specific simile for uselessness.
jokes.explanation: 'Dick at a roller derby' is a vivid, specific, and genuinely funny image for uselessness — the juxtaposition of genital anatomy and a specific sporting event creates a memorable insult. Veep's insult catalog at functional-to-good quality.

jokes.text: Good morning. Selina Meyer present. We will now vote on the next item. / These vultures wanna raise the price of some new cancer drug. / Aye.
jokes.setup: Selina is on the phone for a pharmaceutical board vote while simultaneously doing a memoir interview.
jokes.punchline: She votes to raise the price of a cancer drug while barely listening, mid-sentence, with her only engagement being a perfunctory 'Aye.'
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of Selina's casual 'Aye' to raising cancer drug prices while telling intimate family stories is a sharp satirical knife. The casualness with which she causes harm is the entire joke — and it's very much a Veep kind of joke.

jokes.text: My daddy was a very good family man, okay? He was charismatic, he was gregarious, he was somewhat peculiar, yeah... but all interesting people are, okay?!
jokes.setup: Mike is cautiously suggesting that details about Selina's father don't add up.
jokes.punchline: Selina's defense of her father escalates from legitimate virtues to 'somewhat peculiar' — which she immediately rationalizes — revealing her denial as the details mount.
jokes.explanation: The escalating defense that includes 'peculiar' as a positive attribute reveals Selina's motivated reasoning. Solid character comedy but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Third vote. / Nay. / But it's for discounted prescriptions for the elderly. / Oh. Nay.
jokes.setup: The board vote series continues — this time for discounted prescriptions for the elderly.
jokes.punchline: Selina votes Nay without knowing what it's for, then when told it's to help the elderly, confirms her Nay anyway.
jokes.explanation: The triple-vote escalation pays off perfectly here. The first two were morally bad things she voted yes on reflexively — now she votes no on something good without knowing, and when informed, doubles down with 'Oh. Nay.' The 'Oh' is what makes it: a brief moment of humanity immediately suppressed. The best execution of the running gag.

jokes.text: I mean, do I fuck her? The entire town thinks I am anyway. / You're my mom's favorite couple. / Sort of like Indiana Jones, I guess. Digging my way through the ancient ruins, hacking my way past the crazy cobwebs and snakes and shit, hoping my face doesn't melt when I open her up.
jokes.setup: Dan is deciding whether to pursue a sexual relationship with Jane McCabe for professional benefit.
jokes.punchline: He extends the metaphor to Indiana Jones, with Jane's body cast as 'ancient ruins' and 'cobwebs' — a grotesque extended metaphor about older women.
jokes.explanation: The Indiana Jones extended metaphor for potentially sleeping with an older woman is both characteristic of Dan's cynicism and genuinely inventive in its gross specificity. 'Hoping my face doesn't melt when I open her up' (a Raiders of the Lost Ark callback) is a strong final beat.

jokes.text: Zero anal access. How's that for a new deal?
jokes.setup: Amy explains that Selina's wax figure has been moved to a bench next to FDR.
jokes.punchline: 'How's that for a new deal?' — the FDR New Deal referenced as a pun about the physical inaccessibility of Selina's wax figure.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke — the FDR New Deal as a punchline for the very specific circumstance of Selina's wax figure positioning requires political knowledge, anatomical bluntness, and wordplay all working simultaneously. The economy is superb. One of the episode's strongest jokes.

jokes.text: Not just the beans. Also, the franks.
jokes.setup: Amy describes tourists resting their balls on the Selina wax figure.
jokes.punchline: Dan clarifies it's not just the testicles but also the penis — delivered in deli-counter terminology ('beans' and 'franks').
jokes.explanation: The euphemistic escalation from 'beans' to 'also, the franks' — using deli vocabulary — is a wonderfully specific and absurd word choice. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Or we could trade places, and you could be in charge of a library that doesn't exist for a president who is currently more famous for being a jizz sock for Times Square tourists than for serving her country.
jokes.setup: Amy is losing patience with Dan's hesitation about sleeping with Jane McCabe.
jokes.punchline: 'A jizz sock for Times Square tourists' as a description of Selina Meyer's current public image — a career-defining summary delivered in bitter exasperation.
jokes.explanation: A superb callback-escalation that synthesizes the entire wax figure subplot into a single devastating phrase. 'Jizz sock for Times Square tourists' is the logical endpoint of the tourist-pose degradation thread, and Amy's exasperated delivery frames it as a rallying cry rather than a joke. Highly quotable and satisfying as both comedy and character work.

jokes.text: That is the most beautiful thing I've ever seen.
jokes.setup: Amy and Gary show Dan photos of tourists posing lewdly with Selina's wax figure.
jokes.punchline: Dan's sincere appreciation — 'the most beautiful thing I've ever seen' — about lewd tourist photos of his former boss's likeness.
jokes.explanation: Dan's sociopathic delight at Selina's humiliation — expressed as aesthetic appreciation — is perfectly character-integrated. The straightforwardness of the delivery is what makes it.

jokes.text: My daddy actually tried to pull her wig off because he didn't like her Broadway show.
jokes.setup: Selina recounts the story of her father making Ethel Merman cry.
jokes.punchline: The means of expressing theater criticism was attempted wig removal — an absurdly physical response to a Broadway show.
jokes.explanation: A good character anecdote — it paints Selina's father as a colorful, charismatic scoundrel and gives context for the DNA she inherited. The specificity of Ethel Merman and wig-pulling as a theater critique is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: No, he was away on business. / No, that's just what we told her when she was a little girl. But you knew that, right?
jokes.setup: Selina has left. Uncle George clarifies the true story of her father's death — he didn't die away on business.
jokes.punchline: The 'business' was actually dying in the barn with his secretary — the entire story Selina just told on camera for her memoir was a cover story she was never informed had been debunked.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured revelation scene. The 'but you knew that, right?' addressed to Mike — who absolutely did not know — is the perfect delivery mechanism for destroying everything Selina just confided. The structural payoff on the earlier secretary-at-2AM detail is excellent.

jokes.text: Died right on top of her. / What a way to go. / She was built, my God.
jokes.setup: Uncle George reveals that Selina's father died during sex with his secretary Barbara.
jokes.punchline: George's 'What a way to go' and 'She was built, my God' — treating his friend's adulterous sex-death as an occasion for admiration.
jokes.explanation: Uncle George's gleeful, lecherous reminiscence about the secretary's physique immediately after describing her role in the death-by-sex is excellent dark comedy. 'She was built, my God' has the authentic quality of an old man's inappropriate memory.

jokes.text: She had to go to the loony bin after that. Your mother paid for it. She always referred to it as 'the spa.'
jokes.setup: Barbara, the secretary who died under Selina's father, had a breakdown.
jokes.punchline: Selina's mother paid for the secretary's psychiatric care and called it 'the spa' — the euphemism revealing her mother's characteristic coded cruelty and pragmatism.
jokes.explanation: The 'spa' euphemism for psychiatric care — paid for by the betrayed wife for her husband's mistress — is a rich character detail. It tells you everything about Selina's mother's psychological organization: controlling the narrative by controlling the vocabulary.

jokes.text: Your mother wasn't gonna bail him out anymore.
jokes.setup: Selina is devastated to learn her mother sold Chicklet.
jokes.punchline: George reveals it was actually her father who sold the horse to pay off the IRS — the mother didn't sell Chicklet, the father did, to pay for his infidelities' financial fallout.
jokes.explanation: A strong callback reversal — Selina's earlier joke about her mother (she hated animals she couldn't eat or wear) is subverted: the mother didn't sell Chicklet out of coldness, the father did to pay the IRS. The recontextualization enriches both earlier and current comedy.

jokes.text: You know, she had no children of her own, you know, because your dad was always taking her to the doctor.
jokes.setup: Uncle George is describing Barbara the secretary's love of buying Selina snow globes.
jokes.punchline: The 'because your dad was always taking her to the doctor' — the implication being abortions, delivered by Uncle George as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: Delivered as a warm anecdote about snow globes, this is a masterclass in implicit dark comedy. Uncle George doesn't state the implication — the audience fills it in. The casualness of the delivery is what makes it land.

jokes.text: Barbara loved buying you those. You know, she had no children of her own, you know, because your dad was always taking her to the doctor.
jokes.setup: George reveals that Selina's beloved snow globes were bought by Barbara, the secretary.
jokes.punchline: Barbara 'had no children of her own because your dad was always taking her to the doctor' — an extraordinary euphemism for what happens when you have unprotected sex with a married man.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary piece of compression. George's 'always taking her to the doctor' is a masterwork of understated euphemism for repeated abortions, delivered as a fond memory. The cruelty is all in what's not said. One of the episode's strongest moments for earned dark comedy.

jokes.text: It's fun to drive again, especially drunk.
jokes.setup: Selina is enjoying driving on the estate.
jokes.punchline: The casual 'especially drunk' appended to 'fun to drive again' — a throwaway that reveals Selina is drinking and driving while emotionally processing devastating family revelations.
jokes.explanation: 'Especially drunk' is a perfectly placed throwaway — maximum comedy for minimum words. The casual acknowledgment that she is drunk while driving, on a public road, while being driven by Mike, is a strong character beat.

jokes.text: He was a cheater and a liar! He was a horse thief! I was stupid little Daddy's girl!
jokes.setup: Selina sees her childhood photo with her father and has a breakdown.
jokes.punchline: 'He was a horse thief!' — technically accurate (he sold Chicklet), but wildly operatic in its Old West framing, treating a tax-evasion horse sale as frontier criminality.
jokes.explanation: 'He was a horse thief!' as the culminating accusation in a list of serious crimes is both funny and structurally satisfying — it's technically accurate but tonally absurd given the other items on the list. Selina's dramatic escalation is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: He was a cheater and a liar! He was a horse thief! I was stupid little Daddy's girl!
jokes.setup: Selina, in the barn/stable, begins an emotional breakdown about her father.
jokes.punchline: The progression: cheater → liar → horse thief. The horse thief accusation is both literally accurate (he sold her horse) and an absurdly old-fashioned crime category that undercuts the gravity.
jokes.explanation: The 'horse thief' label is the perfect culmination of the Chicklet storyline — Selina's father literally stole her horse (by selling it without her knowledge). The term is both archaic and emotionally accurate. 'Stupid little Daddy's girl' is also a genuinely effective line showing Selina's self-awareness breaking through.

jokes.text: Pay me! Pay me, bitch! / Yeah, you pay him, you bitch!
jokes.setup: Selina and Mike are smashing up the old stable/office in a cathartic rage.
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'Pay me, bitch!' to the desk — and Selina's cheerleading — turning therapeutic anger into something absurd and slightly unhinged.
jokes.explanation: Mike raging at furniture about being underpaid while smashing it up is a great physical comedy beat. The unexpected 'Pay me' shift from therapeutic to transactional is funny, and Selina's immediate support ('Yeah, you pay him') is a warm comic moment between them.

jokes.text: Wait, stop! This is a pencil holder that I made for my daddy when I was at camp.
jokes.setup: Selina and Mike are in a destructive rampage through the barn.
jokes.punchline: Mid-rampage, Selina stops to protect a childmade pencil holder — the emotional whiplash from rage to tender protectiveness over a camp craft project.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed interruption of the destruction scene. The fact that it was made 'for my daddy' — the man she was just destroying the room over — adds an ironic layer to the protectiveness. Perfect structural placement.

jokes.text: Why do shoes cost so much money?! / He wasn't so perfect! / He loved them and he was fucking them! / I wish I had peed first. / Why do I always wait so long to pee? / Oh, my God. / I'm a grown man! / I shouldn't eat when I'm full!
jokes.setup: The therapeutic destruction is reaching its peak.
jokes.punchline: Selina's earnest emotional outbursts are intercut with Mike's completely mundane complaints ('I wish I had peed first,' 'I'm a grown man! I shouldn't eat when I'm full!'), deflating the drama.
jokes.explanation: The intercutting of Selina's genuine emotional catharsis with Mike's pedestrian self-reproaches ('Why do shoes cost so much money?', 'I shouldn't eat when I'm full') is a masterpiece of tonal contrast. Mike's oblivious participation in a ritual he doesn't understand, venting about completely unrelated grievances, is hilarious character comedy.

jokes.text: My mom was right and I ignored her, and I married my fucking father.
jokes.setup: After the cathartic destruction, Selina processes the revelations about her father.
jokes.punchline: The psychoanalytic cliché of 'I married my father' delivered by a former President of the United States as a genuine breakthrough — with 'fucking' adding both emphasis and a slightly unintended second meaning.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on the irony of the cliché being true — Selina married Andrew, who is indeed her father's spiritual double (charming, unfaithful, financially reckless). The 'fucking' is doing double duty as both an intensifier and a literal descriptor of her father's behavior.

jokes.text: This is the story of the gifted girl who triumphed over her parents' toxic marriage to become an American icon! Don't you see? I had no choice but to go into politics and be extraordinary and a sex symbol.
jokes.setup: Selina has just had a genuine emotional breakdown among burning ruins.
jokes.punchline: She immediately reframes the disaster as the origin story for her greatness — and her sex symbol status — delivered in pitch-perfect motivational delusion.
jokes.explanation: Selina's narcissistic transformation of family trauma into personal myth is a perfect Veep character moment. 'I had no choice but to be extraordinary and a sex symbol' is the capsule of her entire psychology — everything is a mandate for her greatness. High character integration.

jokes.text: Mike crashed my car. He's completely wasted. / I made this at camp. / Look, I made it at camp.
jokes.setup: Emergency services have arrived at the burning barn.
jokes.punchline: Selina reports Mike's drunk car crash as a footnote while Mike — concussed or drunk — keeps fixating on his pencil holder.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the pencil holder is excellent — Mike has apparently grabbed it amid the chaos and is fixating on it while possibly concussed. Selina's flat 'He's completely wasted' delivery against the burning backdrop is perfect bathos. The repetition of 'I made it at camp' as a mantra sells Mike's disorientation.

jokes.text: Hey, so, Danny, before I have a morning bagel, I just wanna make sure you don't wanna fuck that, too.
jokes.setup: Jane and Dan are on air together; they've just wrapped.
jokes.punchline: Jane's 'just making sure you don't want to fuck that too' — referring to her bagel — is delivered with broadcast professionalism turned into a cutting territorial insult.
jokes.explanation: Jane's controlled venom is exactly her character. The bagel as the object of the insult is inspired — it's both absurd and precise. Her power is visible in how casually she delivers the threat.

jokes.text: Dan, if I stop being fuckable, then I am Grandma, and if I'm Grandma, I will be replaced by two tits with a degree from American University over there.
jokes.setup: Jane is confronting Dan about the affair rumors — making clear she's aware of the industry's treatment of women.
jokes.punchline: 'Two tits with a degree from American University' as a description of her eventual replacement — cynical, self-aware, and darkly funny. The callback to American University lands as a bonus.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments. Jane's brutal self-awareness about her position as a female anchor — measured in fuckability, replaced by younger models from low-prestige schools — is both sharp satire and great character work. The callback to American University from the opening scene gives it an extra structural payoff. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: It's you. You're the one telling people. / Smart and handsome.
jokes.setup: Jane accuses Dan of being the one spreading the affair rumors.
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'Smart and handsome' — a two-word self-congratulation for being caught, delivered as if being identified as a manipulator is a compliment.
jokes.explanation: 'Smart and handsome' as a response to being caught in a manipulation is perfectly Dan — unrepentant, self-satisfied, and vain. Economy is near-perfect. Great character button.

jokes.text: Why do we always have to raise the ceiling? / So you don't scrape your head, you giant barbecue fork.
jokes.setup: Jonah mishears or misunderstands 'debt ceiling' as a literal ceiling.
jokes.punchline: 'So you don't scrape your head, you giant barbecue fork' — using Jonah's absurd height as the punchline.
jokes.explanation: 'Giant barbecue fork' is a truly inspired insult for a very tall person — the visual of a barbecue fork (long, thin, pointed, a kitchen implement) applied to a human being is both absurd and precisely right for Jonah's gangly physique. The setup of Jonah not understanding the debt ceiling is also perfectly character-integrated. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You need to get rid of your glasses. They make you look smart. / Okay. / Voters hate that.
jokes.setup: Campaign advisors are grooming Jonah's image.
jokes.punchline: The instruction to remove glasses because they make him 'look smart' — and 'voters hate that' — is a sharp political satire beat about anti-intellectualism in contemporary politics.
jokes.explanation: 'They make you look smart. Voters hate that.' is a sharp, economical political satire joke. With Jonah as the candidate it gains an extra ironic layer — he doesn't actually look smart regardless.

jokes.text: Come on, if the other congressmen see me with a cheat sheet, they're gonna think I'm a fucking idiot. / You can't unring that bell.
jokes.setup: Jonah resists using a color-coded chart to help him vote correctly.
jokes.punchline: Moe's 'you can't unring that bell' — delivered deadpan — means his reputation as an idiot is already established. The ring has been rung.
jokes.explanation: The use of the idiom in its most literal sense — 'you can't unring that bell' meaning his idiocy is already public knowledge — is an excellent straight-faced dagger. The economy is perfect.

jokes.text: No, I'm gonna look like that old popcorn homo. / You'll look like a school teacher or a small-town grocer — good, solid New England professions. / Yeah, or like a nation of Islam badass.
jokes.setup: Shawnee wants Jonah to switch to bow ties.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'old popcorn homo' for Orville Redenbacher is an absurd brand insult; Shawnee's straight reframe ('school teacher, small-town grocer') is reasonable; Jonah escalating to 'Nation of Islam badass' as a positive alternative shows his complete misunderstanding of his brand.
jokes.explanation: Three characters, three escalating visions for the bow tie. 'Old popcorn homo' as Orville Redenbacher is a specific and original insult; the Nation of Islam escalation shows Jonah's self-image is wildly divorced from reality. The structure is tight.

jokes.text: Come on, if the other congressmen see me with a cheat sheet, they're gonna think I'm a fucking idiot. / You can't unring that bell.
jokes.setup: Jonah resists using a cheat sheet out of image concerns.
jokes.punchline: 'You can't unring that bell' — the implication being that everyone already knows Jonah is a fucking idiot, so a cheat sheet is irrelevant.
jokes.explanation: The 'you can't unring that bell' lands perfectly because of what it implies about Jonah's established reputation. The idiom is used correctly but in an entirely unexpected direction.

jokes.text: Why can't we just write it on my hand like we used to? That worked and I felt good about myself.
jokes.setup: Jonah is resisting the color-coded chart as too embarrassing.
jokes.punchline: 'That worked and I felt good about myself' — Jonah's nostalgic attachment to having notes written on his hand, described as a source of self-esteem.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's emotional attachment to hand-written cheat notes and the sense of accomplishment they gave him is a perfect character joke. 'That worked and I felt good about myself' is deeply quotable and works as both an insight into Jonah's psychology and a political commentary on competence.

jokes.text: No, I'm gonna look like that old popcorn homo.
jokes.setup: Jonah is told he's switching to bow ties.
jokes.punchline: 'Old popcorn homo' as an image for bow-tie-wearing men — oddly specific and revealing of Jonah's particular mental taxonomy.
jokes.explanation: 'Old popcorn homo' is a genuinely strange insult image — the specificity of 'popcorn' is inexplicable and therefore funny. Character-specific to Jonah's oddly vivid, slightly antiquated vocabulary.

jokes.text: Yeah, or like a nation of Islam badass.
jokes.setup: Advisors describe bow ties as making Jonah look like 'a school teacher or a small-town grocer.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's alternative positive association for bow ties is Nation of Islam — a genuinely unlikely image for a tall, goofy white congressman.
jokes.explanation: Jonah fantasizing about looking like a Nation of Islam minister while being the least likely person to achieve that aesthetic is a sharp absurdist beat. His self-image is hilariously deluded.

jokes.text: How do small-town child molesters poll?
jokes.setup: Kent notes that 'small-town grocers poll exceptionally well for probity.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's next logical inquiry — how do small-town child molesters poll — as if he's working through the demographic spectrum.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's polling question is a perfect character-comedy dark joke. The straight-faced inquiry about child molesters in the context of electoral demographics is both shocking and completely Jonah. The economy is outstanding — five words after a setup about polling numbers.

jokes.text: Jobs for wetlands duck-fuckers who are never gonna vote for Jonah.
jokes.setup: Jonah protests that voting against a pork barrel bill will cost 300 jobs.
jokes.punchline: 'Wetlands duck-fuckers' as the description of the constituency those 300 jobs serve — implying they're an environmental labor class utterly opposed to Jonah.
jokes.explanation: 'Wetlands duck-fuckers' is a vivid, specific piece of political invective that perfectly captures dismissal of environmental-sector labor. Characteristically Veep in its precision of contempt.

jokes.text: If I vote no, can I turn your drylands into wetlands?
jokes.setup: Jonah is deciding whether to vote no on the wetlands bill after being told it will play well.
jokes.punchline: Jonah attempts a sexual pun using the 'wetlands/drylands' language of the bill — directed at his female advisor.
jokes.explanation: A weak pun that works primarily as a Jonah character beat — his inability to resist making an embarrassing sexual joke even in a business context. The concept is familiar but character-specific execution saves it from being entirely flat.

jokes.text: Well, the female form is a formidable adversary. / Well, ironically, I have bigger tits.
jokes.setup: Kent delivers a philosophical comment on dealing with Shawnee's influence on Jonah.
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'ironically, I have bigger tits' — delivered flatly — is both a self-deprecating comment about his body and a dismissal of Kent's pomposity.
jokes.explanation: Ben's flat self-deprecation is perfectly timed. The 'ironically' is the key word — it's doing a lot of work, framing the admission as a logical observation rather than a complaint. Ben's delivery voice, even in print, sells this completely.

jokes.text: Well, ironically, I have bigger tits.
jokes.setup: Selina's advisor comments on 'the female form as a formidable adversary' regarding campaign dynamics.
jokes.punchline: Selina interjects that she has bigger breasts than the female challenger — delivered with dry one-upmanship.
jokes.explanation: A quick Selina character beat. The casual competitive boast about breast size as a political calculation is funny in its specificity, though not among the episode's stronger jokes.

jokes.text: What does Rumpleforeskin want?
jokes.setup: Mike reports that Sherman Tanz is calling in a favor for supporting Selina's library.
jokes.punchline: 'Rumpleforeskin' as Selina's nickname for Sherman Tanz — a portmanteau of Rumpelstiltskin and foreskin, implying he's a devious little man and something anatomically unpleasant.
jokes.explanation: 'Rumpleforeskin' is an exceptional piece of Veep insult craftsmanship. It combines a fairytale villain reference (a manipulative little creature who makes impossible demands), an anatomical vulgarity, and operates as a portmanteau all in one word. Perfect economy. This is Veep's insult comedy at its creative peak.

jokes.text: And what is his son's name? / Prescott. / Prescott. Who does he think he's trying to kid?
jokes.setup: Selina is writing a letter of recommendation for Tanz's son.
jokes.punchline: 'Prescott. Who does he think he's trying to kid?' — the absurdly WASP-y name as evidence of Tanz's social pretension.
jokes.explanation: A quick class-comedy beat — Tanz naming his son Prescott to claim old-money status is exposed by Selina's snobbishly acute social radar. Efficient and character-specific.

jokes.text: Sure. You want me to take out that I worked for you diligently for 20 years? / Diligently? I... I'm not sure I concur with that.
jokes.setup: Mike is rewriting the letter of recommendation he wrote for Ellen (now being repurposed for Prescott).
jokes.punchline: Selina challenges the word 'diligently' as an accurate description of Mike's 20 years of service — while making him do exactly that.
jokes.explanation: Selina quibbling with 'diligently' while exploiting Mike's labor is a neat piece of situational irony. The moment where she adds 'I'm not sure I concur' to a description of Mike that he himself wrote is quietly devastating.

jokes.text: She's gonna get into the school anyway. / She's Asian. / Sure.
jokes.setup: Mike tries to assuage Selina's guilt about redirecting the letter.
jokes.punchline: Mike's casually racist rationale — that Mike's daughter will get in because she's Asian — delivered and accepted as normal calculation.
jokes.explanation: A dark joke about model minority stereotypes in elite admissions, played as a casual throwaway. Veep uses its characters' casual racism as character revelation — the flatness of the delivery is the joke.

jokes.text: It's like she doesn't hear me or even see me. And anytime she does, all she cares about is tearing me down, and it's like she's trying to ruin everything.
jokes.setup: Catherine is in therapy complaining about her mother.
jokes.punchline: The irony is that Selina is literally right there in the room, having a separate conversation — Catherine is being accurately observed but is oblivious to the meta-comedy of complaining about Selina's inattention in Selina's presence.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony of Catherine's therapy complaint being literally demonstrated in real-time — Selina not listening — is well-constructed. Situational comedy that makes a structural point about the Selina-Catherine dynamic.

jokes.text: Okay, Catherine, I know I'm not supposed to interrupt you, but do you realize these are the same complaints you've had for 15 years? / Yeah, but they're not exactly the same. I think that things have changed. / Okay, Catherine, sit up. You're slouching. / And what is that on your sweater?
jokes.setup: Catherine is in therapy with her mother present, trying to communicate her ongoing issues.
jokes.punchline: The therapist attempts a gentle confrontation, Catherine pushes back, and Selina immediately proves Catherine's point by critiquing her posture and sweater.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional structural comedy. The therapist's 'these are the same complaints you've had for 15 years' is designed to challenge Catherine, but Selina's immediate pivot to 'sit up, you're slouching / what is that on your sweater' proves every single complaint Catherine just made. The structure — complaint, challenge, instant proof — is airtight. High rewatch value because the joke works on multiple levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: What's happening with my wax statue? / Gary and I were able to have you placed on the replica of the Truman Balcony. So, it's very, very high up. And safe. And you are encased in glass from the front and the back... It's very safe.
jokes.setup: Selina asks for a status update on the wax figure — this is the final resolution of the episode-long wax figure thread.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'very, very high up. And safe... It's very safe' over-emphasis on safety signals that Selina's figure has been quarantined rather than celebrated — the 'solution' is imprisonment.
jokes.explanation: A strong running gag payoff — the 'solution' to Selina's sexually-harassed wax figure is to encase it in glass on a high balcony. Amy's repeated 'very safe' is a tell that 'safe' is a euphemism for 'inaccessible for good reason.' Good structural resolution.

jokes.text: So, people were trying to have sex with my statue. / Yeah. / Domestic terrorism. / Still, it's better than standing next to Gerald Ford.
jokes.setup: Selina processes the full reality of what tourists were doing with her wax figure.
jokes.punchline: After declaring tourist sexual contact 'domestic terrorism,' Selina's immediate consolation is that it beats being next to Ford — her self-esteem metrics are genuinely alarming.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful callback closer for the wax figure thread. 'Domestic terrorism' for tourist molestation is excellent, but the real punchline is Selina finding comfort in the fact that public sexual assault of her effigy is preferable to proximity to Gerald Ford. Perfect character integration — Selina's status hierarchy is intact even in degradation.

jokes.text: So, people were trying to have sex with my statue. / Yeah. / Domestic terrorism. / Still, it's better than standing next to Gerald Ford.
jokes.setup: Selina processes the revelation about her wax figure.
jokes.punchline: Classifying tourist sexual antics as 'domestic terrorism' — and then immediately conceding it's still better than Ford — is a perfect Selina priorities moment.
jokes.explanation: Two punchlines in quick succession — 'domestic terrorism' and 'better than standing next to Gerald Ford.' The Ford callback lands especially well as the capper to the entire wax figure arc. Selina's priorities (prestige over dignity) are perfectly captured.

jokes.text: See, when people get near this, they just wanna do it. / Oh, yeah. / Thank God you're not trying to do me all the time. / Right?
jokes.setup: Selina and Gary discuss why people sexually engage with the wax figure.
jokes.punchline: Selina says 'thank God you're not trying to do me all the time' to Gary — which is either innocent or a deeply uncomfortable acknowledgment of Gary's devotion to her, and Gary's 'Right?' suggests he also processes this as a relief rather than an insult.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly uncomfortable end beat. Gary's 'Right?' — agreeing that it's good he's not pursuing her — is both the character-correct response and a slightly off response that lands as its own joke. The entire exchange plays on Gary's ambiguous devotion in a way that rewards long-time viewers.

jokes.text: See, when people get near this, they just wanna do it. / Oh, yeah. / Thank God you're not trying to do me all the time. / Right?
jokes.setup: Selina is looking at her wax figure and commenting on its appeal.
jokes.punchline: Selina thanks Gary for not constantly wanting to have sex with her — which is darkly funny given Gary's well-established adoring, quasi-romantic devotion to Selina.
jokes.explanation: For viewers who know the show, this is a deeply ironic joke — Gary's entire relationship with Selina is characterized by a worshipful, non-sexual (but emotionally total) devotion. His 'Right?' in response is the perfect beat from someone who may actually be suppressing exactly that. Great for rewatchers especially.

jokes.text: Dominoes.
jokes.setup: Gary asks 'You all right?' after the wax figure conversation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's one-word response — 'Dominoes' — is completely non-sequitur as a reply to 'are you all right?' suggesting either a food craving, an abstract philosophical thought, or simply a word that popped into her head. The joke is entirely in the baffling inappropriateness of the response.
jokes.explanation: A masterful final episode beat. 'Dominoes' as the answer to 'You all right?' is utterly inexplicable, which is precisely why it works — it's either a pizza order, an abstract metaphor, or pure Selina brain. The absolute economy (one word) and the total non-sequitur quality make it a perfect character-comedy button. The episode ends on this, which tells you the writers knew exactly what they had.

tmdb_overview: Selina settles on a location for her presidential library. Dan lands in a gossip column. Mike and Selina work on her book. Jonah tries to cement a big donation. Gary manages a mini-crisis at Madame Tussauds.
insight: 81-point episode rides 59 jokes in 38 minutes, banking on character chaos over setup precision.
